title: Tobacco
prose: To ensure the production of quality tobacco it is imperative that the plants grow quickly and evenly from transplanting to reaping. A correctly balanced fertilization programme is therefore one of the most important links in the tobacco production chain. The management of this programme is of equal importance. PLANT NUTRIENT REMOVAL The following quantities (kg) of macronutrients are removed by a crop of 1 500 kg leaves, according to the Tobacco and Cotton Research Institute. Table 5.11.1. Removal of plant nutrients by 1 ton of leaves (based on a crop of 1 500 kg) From the above table it is evident that different types of tobacco require different quantities of nitrogen for optimum production. It also shows that potassium is a very important nutrient and this element should therefore be available in sufficient quantities. In practice, however, fertilization with macro-nutrients differs from the removal figures. YIELD POTENTIAL The determination of a realistic yield target will depend on several production factors. These include soil, climate (rainfall, temperature, etc.), availability of irrigation water, growing season and probably the most important of all, the farmer. Table 5.11.2. Yield potential for tobacco * "Dryland" refers to situations where moisture has been stored in the soil or supplementary irrigation. SOIL ANALYSIS Input costs for the production of tobacco are very high. To ensure maximum yield it is therefore imperative that there are no weak links in the production chain. Soil analysis is an important requirement in determining a meaningful fertilizer recommendation for a specific field. The interpretation of the soil analysis should be done by a qualified agronomist. CROP ROTATION A sound crop rotation system is necessary to maintain optimum production on a specific field. Sound rotation implies a system by which soil fertility is increased, erosion is decreased, soil texture is improved and nematode populations are kept under control. The ideal system is to cultivate tobacco on a specific field every fourth year, with the other three years allocated to cultivating a nematode resistant grass pasture such as Rhodes Katambora. The availability and cost of irrigable land places significant limits on the implementation of the ideal system. If tobacco has to be planted on a specific field every second year, it should preferably be alternated with a grain crop. Mono-culture of tobacco on the same piece of land is definitely not advised, because of the resultant problems. LIMING Soil acidity The ideal pH (KCl) is between 5.0 and 6.0. If the soil analysis indicates that the pH is too low, the type and quantity of lime to be applied will be dictated by the calcium and magnesium status of the soil. Lime should be applied as early as possible, preferably during the preceding season. Table 5.11.3. Type of lime recommended for tobacco according to the Mg-analysis MACRO-ELEMENTS Calcium (Ca) Sufficient calcium in the soil will provide a strongly developed plant with a good colour and a strong root system. Sufficient calcium will also enhance the uptake of both potassium and magnesium, and has a positive effect on the nicotine content of the tobacco. The desired open-grained leaf is also promoted by good Ca-levels, but it is not the only contributing factor. A calcium deficiency will cause the younger leaves to be deformed (resembles insect damage), the growth point will be inhibited and the root system will be poorly developed. The leaf will be more tightly grained. In instances where the pH of the soil is already optimal but the Ca-status remains low, this can be rectified by the addition of gypsum. Magnesium (Mg) Sufficient magnesium is essential for a tobacco plant with a healthy green colour and no yellowing. It promotes the uptake of nitrogen and the uptake and transport of phosphorus. A Mg-deficiency will lead to a shortage of chlorophyll and this will result in yellowing occurring in the interveinal areas of the older leaves. Nitrogen uptake will be inhibited, and this will result in a low nicotine content. Magnesium deficiencies may occur on highly leached (acid) sandy soils or where excessive potassium has been applied. It also occurs frequently during wet seasons. An application of dolomitic lime should supply sufficient magnesium. Magnesium deficiencies may, however, also occur where the soil acidity has already been rectified but the Mg-status remains low. Where a Mg-deficiency is expected it can be corrected by the application of 25 kg Mg ha -1 in the form of magnesium sulphate at an early growth stage. Nitrogen (N) Nitrogen promotes good vegetative growth of the tobacco plant, with large healthy leaves with a good colour and high nicotine content. Sufficient nitrogen enhances the uptake of phosphate and potassium, and also increases the moisture content in the leaves to promote ripening. The increased vegetative growth is important as it is directly related to yield. Excessive nitrogen retards ripening and results in large thick leaves that do not colour in flue-cured tobacco production. In air-cured tobacco it results in green curing. This lowers the quality of the crop. Too little nitrogen will result in stunted plants with small leaves and general yellowing, and a very poor yield. The tobacco plant's N-requirement varies from relatively little in flue-cured tobacco to three times that amount in dark air-cured tobacco and Burley tobacco. In the case of both flue-cured and Burley tobacco, all the nitrogen should be applied as soon as possible after planting. In other types of tobacco the last top-dressing is at the initiation of the flower bud. This corresponds with the ideal topping stage. Tobacco quality is strongly influenced by the availability of ammonium and nitrate forms for uptake. An excessive uptake of ammonium nitrogen may decrease the quality due to a lower uptake of calcium, magnesium and especially potassium. Nitrate nitrogen is therefore preferred for tobacco production, as it is immediately available to the plant. Although the tobacco plant can utilise both forms of nitrogen, the nitrate form is preferred for quality considerations. The nitrification process in the soil, from ammonium to nitrate, can be retarded by soil fumigation (which is absolutely essential), low pH of the soil, cool climatic conditions and excessively wet and/or poorly aerated soils. It is therefore advisable for the basic fertilization to contain at least 50% of the nitrogen as nitrate, and top-dressings only in the nitrate form, if at all practical. Many producers of air-cured tobacco under irrigation report good results with up to 300 kg N ha -1 . Recommendations will also differ according to the cultivar. It is advisable to consult a specialist agronomist for regional and cultivar differences. Table 5.11.4. Guidelines for N-fertilization of flue-cured tobacco for yield potentials of 3 to 4 t ha -1 Table 5.11.5. Guidelines for N-fertilization of dark air-cured tobacco Phosphorus (P) The P-requirement of the tobacco plant is relatively low, but fairly large quantities have to be applied because of the fact that this element is not readily available. Tobacco mainly utilises water-soluble phosphate, and therefore it must be ensured that sufficient water-soluble phosphate is available during the active growing season. If available, rock phosphate should preferably be applied in the preceding season, with water-soluble phosphate applied as soon as practically possible before plant- ing. This is done in either one or two bands during ridging. Sufficient phosphorus promotes cell division, resulting in a well-developed root system. This in turn promotes better nutrient and water uptake. Ripening, good colouring and easier drying of tobacco are also promoted by sufficient phosphate. A P-deficiency results in poor root development, a dark-green colour with retarded ripening, and poor colouring during the curing process. White spots can also develop on the lower leaves. Potassium (K) The tobacco plant's requirement for potassium is greater than for any other element, i.e. about 12 times greater than for phosphorus and 2.5 times greater than for nitrogen. The uptake is high during the initial growing stage, and then decreases gradually towards the topping stage. Table 5.11.6. Guidelines for P-fertilization of all types of tobacco under irrigation Very few, if any, of the soils under tobacco cultivation are capable of providing the plant with sufficient potassium during the peak growing period. Potassium fertilization is therefore essential. Potassium can also be applied as a top- dressing as late as two weeks before topping. Chlorine-free potassium such as potassium nitrate should always be applied because of the chlorine problems associated with tobacco quality. The nitrate in potassium nitrate promotes the uptake of potassium. This makes it the most suitable chlorine free source of potassium for tobacco production. However, potassium sulphate can also be applied. Sufficient potassium is not only conducive to expected growth but also promotes other characteristics required for quality tobacco, for e.g. elasticity, good burning and filling qualities, and drought tolerance. Potassium deficiency is associated with the development of yellow spots which later develop into holes in the leaf. The leaves are inclined to curl down at the sides to form a bowl shape. The leaves appear to have rust, with the dead spots causing holes. The leaf edges are also inclined to die off. The guidelines in this table are based on extracted K-values. There is another school of thought that gives preference to K-ratios to other cations. A specialist agronomist should be consulted in this regard. Table 5.11.7. Guidelines for K-fertilization of all types of tobacco under irrigation Sulphur (S) Sufficient sulphur is as important as any other nutrient for the normal growth of the tobacco plant. American researchers have, however, found that excess sulphur can be just as harmful to the burning qualities of tobacco as is the case with chlorine. These researchers have found that the sulphur content of tobacco fertilizer compounds should preferably not exceed 2% to 3%. MICRO-ELEMENTS Most soils contain sufficient zinc to maintain the requirements of tobacco under cultivation. If a deficiency does occur, this can be overcome by using a chlorine-free Zn fertilizer compound. Boron deficiencies can occur fairly generally on highly leached sandy and gravel soils. Such deficiencies are more acute during low temperatures in the early part of the season and can be rectified by the foliar application of a maximum of 0.8 to 1.0 kg B ha -1 . A foliar application of sodium molybdate (60 to 120 g ha -1 ) at 6 to 8 weeks on low pH-soils may promote nitrate reduction. ACKNOWLEDGEMENT The input of the late Mr. Piet Smit of the Lowveld Tobacco Growers’ Association in revising this chapter is gratefully acknowledged.
